enrichment.short_description: Cloud-based legal operating platform built natively on Salesforce for law firm and corporate legal department case, billing and intake management.
enrichment.seo_title: Litify Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Litify review covering features, pricing, pros, cons and alternatives for law firms evaluating this Salesforce-based legal operating platform.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Litify is a legal operating platform built natively on the Salesforce Platform, designed to help law firms and corporate legal departments manage the full lifecycle of a legal matter, from intake through billing and reporting. The company was founded in 2016 and is headquartered in New York City. Litify positions itself as a platform of action rather than a simple case management tool, emphasizing workflow automation, artificial intelligence and no-code configurability layered on top of Salesforce's CRM, security and integration infrastructure. Because Litify is built on Salesforce, it inherits Salesforce's data model, permissions system and AppExchange ecosystem, which lets firms combine Litify with other Salesforce products, including Salesforce's own Agentforce AI tooling, and with the broader Salesforce partner network. This architecture differentiates Litify from most legal practice management competitors, which are typically standalone SaaS applications built on proprietary stacks. The tradeoff is that Litify implementations usually involve Salesforce-style configuration and onboarding rather than the simple sign-up flows common among smaller legal tech vendors. The core product spans six functional areas: intake management with dynamic questionnaires and client portals, matter management with case workflows and task automation, document management with AI-assisted document generation and PDF editing, time and billing with automated time capture, spend management with expense tracking and invoice review (strengthened by Litify's 2021 acquisition of e-billing vendor LegalStratus), and reporting and analytics through a no-code reporting engine for matter- and firm-level KPIs. Litify markets itself across more than 15 practice areas, with particular strength among personal injury and insurance defense firms that handle high volumes of intakes, as well as immigration practices and in-house corporate legal departments at large enterprises. Reported customers and partners include Salesforce itself, Brooklyn Defenders and USPS's legal function; the company states it serves more than 70,000 legal professionals. Litify does not publish self-serve pricing; prospective customers must request a demo and receive a custom quote, standard practice for Salesforce-native enterprise software where cost depends on user counts, Salesforce license tier and implementation scope. This contrasts with much of the legal practice management market, where vendors like Clio, MyCase and PracticePanther publish per-user monthly pricing starting well under $100. Litify raised institutional funding to build out this positioning, including a $50 million round in 2019 led by Tiger Global Management on top of smaller early rounds in 2017 and 2018, bringing total disclosed funding to roughly $70 million between 2016 and 2023. Litify's principal competitors fall into two groups: the small set of other Salesforce-native legal platforms, and standalone practice management suites such as Clio, MyCase, Filevine, SmartAdvocate and PracticePanther. Litify differentiates itself primarily through the Salesforce foundation, which appeals to larger firms and corporate legal departments that already run Salesforce elsewhere and want a single extensible platform rather than a point solution. Its AI and no-code reporting features aim to reduce the custom-development burden traditionally associated with Salesforce implementations, while the LegalStratus acquisition signals an ambition to own e-billing and spend management rather than relying on third-party integrations. For firms evaluating Litify, the key considerations are firm size and complexity, since it is generally positioned toward mid-size to large firms and enterprise legal departments rather than solo practitioners, existing Salesforce investment, and appetite for a longer, consultative sales and implementation cycle in exchange for deeper customization and platform extensibility. Smaller firms seeking simple, inexpensive case management with rapid self-service onboarding are more likely to be better served by lighter-weight standalone tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Litify is a legal operating platform built on the Salesforce Platform, founded in 2016 and headquartered in New York City. It helps law firms and corporate legal departments manage intake, matter workflows, documents, billing and reporting in a single connected system.

Because it runs natively on Salesforce, Litify inherits Salesforce's CRM data model, security architecture and AppExchange integration ecosystem, distinguishing it from most standalone legal practice management tools built on proprietary stacks.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Litify's core modules cover intake management with dynamic questionnaires and client portals, matter management with automated case workflows, AI-assisted document generation, automated time and billing, spend and e-billing management, and a no-code reporting engine for firm-level KPIs.

The platform serves more than 15 practice areas, with particular strength in personal injury and insurance defense firms handling high intake volumes, as well as corporate legal departments at large enterprises.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Litify does not publish self-serve pricing. Interested organizations must request a demo, and cost is determined by a custom quote based on user count, Salesforce license tier and implementation scope.
This quote-based model is typical of Salesforce-native enterprise software and contrasts with standalone competitors like Clio or MyCase, which publish flat per-user monthly pricing.

features.description: No-code reporting engine delivering real-time KPIs and firm-level insights.

features.title: Salesforce-Native Architecture

features.title: Multi Practice-Area Configurability
features.description: Configurable to support 15-plus legal practice areas from personal injury to corporate legal operations.

pros: Deep Salesforce integration unifies legal operations with existing CRM and business systems
No-code reporting engine reduces reliance on developers for custom dashboards
Broad functional coverage spanning intake through billing in one platform
Strong fit for high-volume intake practices like personal injury and insurance defense
Backed by institutional funding and an established enterprise legal customer base
cons: No published self-serve pricing, requiring a sales demo before cost is known
Salesforce-based implementations typically involve longer onboarding than lightweight standalone tools
May be costly and complex for solo practitioners or very small firms
Total cost of ownership can include separate Salesforce platform licensing
Firms without existing Salesforce experience face a steeper learning curve

faqs.question: What is Litify?
faqs.answer: Litify is a legal operating platform built on the Salesforce Platform that helps law firms and corporate legal departments manage intake, matters, documents, billing and reporting in one system.

faqs.question: Is Litify built on Salesforce?
faqs.answer: Yes, Litify runs natively on the Salesforce Platform, inheriting its CRM, security and integration infrastructure along with the AppExchange ecosystem.

faqs.question: How much does Litify cost?
faqs.answer: Litify does not publish self-serve pricing. Cost is quote-based and depends on user count, Salesforce license tier and implementation scope, available after a sales demo.

faqs.question: Who uses Litify?
faqs.answer: Litify is used by law firms across 15-plus practice areas, notably personal injury and insurance defense firms, as well as corporate legal departments at large enterprises.

faqs.question: How does Litify differ from Clio or MyCase?
faqs.answer: Unlike Clio or MyCase, which are standalone SaaS practice management tools with self-serve pricing, Litify is built on Salesforce and generally targets larger firms and legal departments needing deeper customization.

faqs.question: Did Litify make any acquisitions?
faqs.answer: Yes, Litify acquired e-billing vendor LegalStratus in 2021 to strengthen its spend management and billing capabilities.

enrichment.short_description: Cookieless, GDPR-compliant, open-source web analytics platform from Italy offering an AI-powered, self-hostable alternative to Google Analytics. Not related to Litify.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (self-hosted) or from EUR 8.99/month (Mini cloud plan)
enrichment.seo_title: Litlyx Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Litlyx review of this open-source, cookieless European web analytics tool: features, pricing plans, self-hosting options and comparison to GA4.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Litlyx is a European, privacy-first web analytics platform positioned as a cookieless alternative to Google Analytics, Mixpanel and similar tracking-heavy tools. It should not be confused with Litify, the Salesforce-based legal practice management platform; despite the similar name, the two products serve entirely different markets and have no corporate relationship. Litlyx was founded on October 21, 2024 by Antonio Verdiglione, a software engineer with roughly nine years of experience in games and mobile development, and is headquartered in Rome, Italy, operating as an Italian pre-seed startup backed by the Techstars Transformative World Torino accelerator program. The product's core proposition is speed and privacy: Litlyx claims a sub-30-second setup via a single script tag, and it markets itself as GDPR-compliant because it collects only anonymous, aggregate usage data rather than persistent identifiers, IP addresses or personally identifiable information, which allows sites to skip cookie-consent banners for analytics purposes. The platform tracks standard web analytics metrics such as page views, referrers and visitor counts, but layers on product-analytics-style features more commonly associated with tools like Mixpanel or PostHog: custom event tracking, conversion funnel analysis, and AI-generated one-click reports that summarize web, product, SEO and marketing performance. It supports multi-domain and multi-workspace management so agencies and consultants can consolidate several client sites in one account, and it can generate shareable, branded reports for clients or stakeholders. Architecturally, Litlyx offers both a hosted cloud product and a self-hosted deployment option, with parts of the codebase open source, having accumulated over 1,700 GitHub stars, even though the primary go-to-market is a hosted SaaS. This dual approach, open-source credibility paired with a polished, AI-augmented commercial product, differentiates Litlyx from purely open-source competitors like Plausible and Umami, which generally require more manual configuration to get AI or advanced reporting features, and from closed-source incumbents like Google Analytics 4, which is free but carries privacy and consent-banner overhead that Litlyx is explicitly designed to avoid. Litlyx prices its hosted plans on an annual-billing basis by monthly pageview volume. The Mini plan starts at EUR 8.99 per month and includes 10,000 monthly pageviews with a single workspace and limited AI features. The Basic plan, at EUR 14.99 per month, raises the pageview allowance to 100,000 per month, adds unlimited domains and roughly 50 AI-generated messages per month, and unlocks basic client-facing reports. The Pro plan, at EUR 29.99 per month, supports 350,000 monthly pageviews across two workspaces, 200 AI messages, custom reports and one additional team seat. Self-hosting via Docker is free for organizations willing to run their own infrastructure, which appeals to developers and privacy-sensitive teams wanting zero recurring cost and full data ownership. The target audience skews toward small agencies, freelancers and solopreneurs who manage multiple client websites and want GDPR compliance without consent-management complexity, as well as small and mid-size businesses migrating away from Google Analytics. Its AI reporting features are aimed specifically at agencies producing recurring client-facing performance summaries without manually building dashboards. Litlyx's principal competitors are other privacy-focused analytics tools, including Plausible Analytics, Fathom Analytics, Simple Analytics and Matomo, as well as the self-hosted Liwan project and, at the high end, product-analytics platforms like PostHog and Mixpanel. Litlyx differentiates itself from the privacy-analytics cohort primarily through AI-generated reporting and fast onboarding, and from product-analytics platforms through its lighter weight and lower price point. As a company founded in late 2024, Litlyx is considerably younger and smaller than most named competitors, so prospective customers should weigh its early-stage status against its lower price and modern feature set.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Litlyx is a cookieless, GDPR-compliant web analytics platform founded in October 2024 by Antonio Verdiglione and headquartered in Rome, Italy, backed by Techstars Transformative World Torino.
It should not be confused with Litify, a Salesforce-based legal practice management platform; the two are unrelated products with similar names.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Litlyx offers cookieless tracking, AI-generated one-click reports, custom event and funnel tracking, real-time dashboards, multi-domain workspace management and shareable client-ready reports.
It is available as both a hosted cloud product and a self-hosted, Docker-deployable open-source option, with over 1,700 GitHub stars on its public repository.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Litlyx's hosted plans are billed annually by monthly pageview volume: Mini at EUR 8.99/month for 10,000 pageviews, Basic at EUR 14.99/month for 100,000 pageviews, and Pro at EUR 29.99/month for 350,000 pageviews.
Self-hosting via Docker is completely free for teams willing to manage their own infrastructure and data.

AI-generated reports save time for agencies producing recurring client summaries
Both hosted and self-hosted deployment options available
Very fast, low-friction setup compared to Google Analytics
Competitive, transparent pricing for small sites and agencies

Small team of under 10 employees may limit support responsiveness at scale
Feature set is less mature than long-established analytics platforms
Pageview-based pricing tiers can become costly for high-traffic sites

faqs.question: What is Litlyx?
faqs.answer: Litlyx is a cookieless, GDPR-compliant web analytics platform offering AI-generated reports as a privacy-friendly alternative to Google Analytics.

faqs.question: Is Litlyx the same as Litify?
faqs.answer: No. Litlyx is a web analytics tool and Litify is an unrelated Salesforce-based legal practice management platform; the similar names are coincidental.

faqs.question: Can Litlyx be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, Litlyx offers a free, open-source, Docker-deployable self-hosted version alongside its hosted cloud plans.

faqs.question: Does Litlyx require a cookie consent banner?
faqs.answer: No, because Litlyx does not use cookies or store personally identifiable information, most sites can avoid a cookie consent banner for analytics purposes.

faqs.question: How much does Litlyx cost?
faqs.answer: Hosted plans start at EUR 8.99 per month for 10,000 monthly pageviews, rising to EUR 29.99 per month for 350,000 pageviews; self-hosting is free.

faqs.question: Who founded Litlyx?
faqs.answer: Litlyx was founded in October 2024 by Antonio Verdiglione and is based in Rome, Italy, with backing from the Techstars Transformative World Torino accelerator.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source live chat and customer support platform with voice, video, screen share and multi-channel bot support, self-hosted or cloud-hosted.
enrichment.pricing_model: Open Source / Freemium (self-hosted free, paid cloud hosting and support available)
enrichment.seo_title: Live Helper Chat Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Live Helper Chat review: features, self-hosted pricing, AI bot integrations, voice/video/screen share and how it compares to other live chat tools.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Live Helper Chat is an open-source live chat and customer support platform created by Lithuanian developer Remigijus Kiminas, known in the community by the handle remdex, who built the project out of frustration with the lack of a genuinely good free live-help application. The project traces its public release history back to the early 2010s, with version 1.1, bundled with the first release of a companion desktop client, shipping in January 2013, and it has been under continuous active development since, with the current version, 4.87 as of mid-2026, still receiving regular updates. Kiminas and the project are based in Šiauliai, Lithuania, and the software is maintained largely as an independent open-source effort rather than by a large venture-backed company. Live Helper Chat is built on a PHP and MySQL stack using an MVC architecture, which makes it straightforward to self-host on commodity LAMP-style infrastructure and to extend through its plugin system. Functionally it goes well beyond a basic chat widget: it supports voice calls, video calls and screen sharing directly in the browser, co-browsing, speech-to-text, file uploads and detailed visitor tracking. It integrates with major messaging channels including Telegram, Facebook Messenger, WhatsApp via Twilio, and SMS, and it ships with native mobile apps for iOS and Android so agents can respond on the go. A significant part of its recent development has focused on AI and bot integrations, including support for OpenAI ChatGPT, Rasa, DeepPavlov, Ollama and Google Gemini, allowing site owners to build automated chatbots or AI-assisted agent responses on top of the same platform used for live human chat. The pricing model is dual-track. The core software is free and open source, distributed for self-hosting, and the project explicitly markets its enterprise level features, meaning unlimited operators, unlimited concurrent chats and unlimited departments, as free for everyone rather than gated behind a paid tier, which is unusual in a market where most competitors reserve high-volume or multi-agent features for paid plans. For organizations that do not want to manage their own server, the team also offers managed cloud hosting and paid commercial support as revenue-generating options layered on top of the free core product. In production use, the platform has demonstrated the ability to handle over 10,000 chats per day across dozens of concurrently active operators, indicating it scales well beyond hobbyist use. Because it is self-hosted and fully open source, Live Helper Chat is particularly attractive to organizations with strict data-residency or data-protection requirements, including banks, healthcare organizations, universities and gaming companies that need to keep customer conversation data on infrastructure they control rather than a third-party SaaS vendor's servers. It also appeals to developers and technical teams who want to customize chat behavior, branding or integrations beyond what a closed commercial product would allow. Live Helper Chat's competitors span both the open-source and commercial live-chat markets. On the open-source side, it competes with Chatwoot and Rocket.Chat, both offering similarly self-hostable alternatives to proprietary help desk and chat tools. On the commercial SaaS side, it competes indirectly with LiveChat, LiveAgent, Intercom, Zendesk Chat and Tawk.to. Its key differentiators are the breadth of built-in real-time communication features bundled in rather than requiring third-party add-ons, the lack of artificial seat or volume caps on the free self-hosted version, and its long operating history as a maintained open-source project. The tradeoff is that, unlike hosted SaaS competitors, self-hosting adopters are responsible for their own server provisioning, updates and uptime unless they pay for managed cloud or support options.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Live Helper Chat is an open-source live chat and customer support platform created by Lithuanian developer Remigijus Kiminas, with public releases dating back to at least 2013 and continuous active development since.
Built on a PHP and MySQL MVC architecture, it is self-hostable on standard LAMP infrastructure or available through managed cloud hosting from the project team.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform bundles voice calls, video calls, screen sharing, co-browsing and speech-to-text alongside standard chat, and integrates with Telegram, Facebook Messenger, WhatsApp and SMS.
Recent development has focused on AI bot integrations including OpenAI ChatGPT, Rasa, DeepPavlov, Ollama and Google Gemini for automated and AI-assisted responses.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The core self-hosted software is free and open source, with no artificial caps on operators, concurrent chats or departments, an unusual stance in a market that typically gates these behind paid tiers.
Revenue comes from optional managed cloud hosting and paid commercial support for organizations that prefer not to run their own servers.

pros: Free and fully open source with no artificial feature or seat caps
Bundles voice, video and screen share that competitors often charge extra for
Strong data control appeals to regulated industries like banking and healthcare
Actively maintained with frequent releases since the early 2010s
Extensive AI chatbot integrations including major LLM providers
cons: Self-hosting requires managing PHP/MySQL server infrastructure and updates
Interface and setup are less polished than modern commercial SaaS competitors
Smaller community and ecosystem than larger open-source projects like Chatwoot
Documentation can be less comprehensive than well-funded commercial vendors
Managed cloud hosting option has less brand recognition than mainstream SaaS chat tools

faqs.question: Is Live Helper Chat free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the self-hosted software is free and open source with no caps on operators, concurrent chats or departments; paid managed cloud hosting and support are optional.

faqs.question: What technology is Live Helper Chat built on?
faqs.answer: It is built on a PHP and MySQL stack using an MVC architecture, making it deployable on standard LAMP infrastructure.

faqs.question: Does Live Helper Chat support AI chatbots?
faqs.answer: Yes, it integrates with OpenAI ChatGPT, Rasa, DeepPavlov, Ollama and Google Gemini for building automated or AI-assisted chat responses.

faqs.question: Who created Live Helper Chat?
faqs.answer: It was created by Lithuanian developer Remigijus Kiminas, who maintains the project under the handle remdex.

faqs.question: Can Live Helper Chat handle high volumes of chats?
faqs.answer: Yes, in production it has demonstrated handling over 10,000 chats per day across dozens of concurrently active operators.

faqs.question: Does Live Helper Chat support voice and video calls?
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes built-in voice calls, video calls and screen sharing alongside standard text chat.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source VS Code extension by Ritwick Dey that launches a local dev server with automatic live reload for static and dynamic pages.
enrichment.seo_title: Live Server Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Live Server VS Code extension review: how it works, key features, install stats, and the best alternatives like Live Preview and Five Server.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Live Server is a free, open-source Visual Studio Code extension created by Indian developer Ritwick Dey and first published to the VS Code Marketplace on June 27, 2017. It solves a narrow but extremely common front-end development problem: instead of manually refreshing a browser tab every time an HTML, CSS or JavaScript file changes, Live Server launches a lightweight local development server and automatically reloads, or for CSS-only changes, hot-injects, the browser the moment a watched file is saved. Because the problem it solves is universal to static and simple dynamic web development, the extension has become one of the most widely installed tools in the entire VS Code Marketplace, with more than 80 million installations and a 4.5-star rating across more than 500 reviews. Technically, Live Server works by injecting a small script into served pages that opens a WebSocket connection back to a local Node.js-based server running inside VS Code's extension host; when the server detects a file change, it pushes a message over that WebSocket telling the browser to reload the page or, for a CSS-only change, to swap the stylesheet without a full page reload. The extension can be started with a single click from the VS Code status bar labeled Go Live, via a right-click context menu on any HTML file, through keyboard shortcuts, or through the command palette. It supports configurable ports, a configurable server root, HTTPS, optional CORS headers, multi-root workspace setups, and remote access over a local network so developers can preview a page on a phone or tablet on the same WiFi network. A companion project, the Live Server Web Extension, available as a Chrome browser extension, extends the same live-reload behavior to dynamic backend stacks such as PHP, Node.js and ASP.NET that Live Server's built-in static server cannot serve directly. As an individual open-source project rather than a company, Live Server has no pricing model in the traditional sense: it is entirely free, distributed under the MIT license, and monetized only indirectly, if at all, through the goodwill and reputation it generates for its maintainer. Ritwick Dey originally built and published the extension as a personal project, and it has since attracted contributions from other developers, including Max Schmitt, Joydip Roy and Ayo Adesugba, who have helped maintain it as its install base grew into the tens of millions. Live Server's target audience is front-end developers, students and coding bootcamp learners who are building static sites, prototyping HTML, CSS and JavaScript layouts, or working through web development tutorials and need instant visual feedback without configuring a full bundler-based dev server like Vite or webpack-dev-server. It is especially popular in educational contexts because it requires zero configuration: installing the extension and clicking Go Live is enough to get a working local server, which is not true of most modern JavaScript build tooling. Its main alternatives fall into two categories. Within VS Code, competing or complementary extensions include Live Preview, Microsoft's own first-party live-reload extension bundled by default in newer VS Code releases, and Five Server, a community fork of Live Server with added features. Outside the editor entirely, developers building anything beyond static prototypes typically graduate to framework-native dev servers such as Vite, webpack-dev-server or Parcel, all of which include hot module replacement and are the standard choice for production-oriented JavaScript and TypeScript projects. Live Server's differentiation is its simplicity and zero-configuration philosophy: it deliberately does not attempt to bundle, transpile or manage dependencies, which keeps it lightweight and beginner-friendly but means it is best suited to static or lightly dynamic pages rather than modern component-based application development.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Live Server is a free VS Code extension created by Ritwick Dey and first published in June 2017. It launches a local development server with automatic browser reload whenever a watched file is saved.
With over 80 million installs and a 4.5-star rating, it is one of the most widely used extensions in the VS Code Marketplace, particularly popular among students and front-end beginners.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Live Server uses a WebSocket connection to push reload or CSS hot-injection events to the browser the instant a file changes, started with a single click from the status bar or a keyboard shortcut.
It supports configurable ports, HTTPS, CORS, multi-root workspaces and remote LAN access, with a companion browser extension enabling live reload for dynamic PHP, Node.js and ASP.NET pages.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Live Server is entirely free and distributed under the MIT license as an open-source project with no paid tiers, subscriptions or in-app purchases.
It is maintained by its original creator alongside community contributors, with no company or commercial entity behind it.

features.title: One-Click Local Server
features.description: Start or stop a local dev server instantly from the VS Code status bar Go Live button.

features.title: Automatic Live Reload
features.description: Browser reloads automatically, or CSS hot-injects, whenever a watched file is saved.

features.title: WebSocket-Based Sync
features.description: Uses a WebSocket channel between the editor and browser to push reload events in real time.

features.title: Configurable Server Settings
features.description: Customizable port, server root, HTTPS and CORS support.

features.title: Multi-Root Workspace Support
features.description: Works across multi-root VS Code workspaces for larger projects.

features.title: Remote LAN Preview
features.description: Accessible over a local network for testing on phones and tablets on the same WiFi.

features.title: Dynamic Page Support via Companion Extension
features.description: Live Server Web Extension adds live reload for PHP, Node.js and ASP.NET backends.

features.title: Zero Configuration Setup
features.description: Works immediately after installation with no build tooling or config files required.

pros: Completely free and open source under the MIT license
Extremely popular with over 80 million installs and strong reviews
Zero configuration required, ideal for beginners and quick prototyping
Lightweight compared to full bundler-based dev servers
Supports HTTPS, CORS and remote network access for mobile testing
cons: Not suited to modern component-based frameworks needing hot module replacement
No built-in bundling, transpiling or dependency management
Maintained as a side project, so update cadence can be inconsistent
Overlaps with Microsoft's own bundled Live Preview extension, causing some confusion
Dynamic backend support requires installing a separate companion browser extension

faqs.question: What does the Live Server VS Code extension do?
faqs.answer: It launches a local development server and automatically reloads the browser whenever a watched HTML, CSS or JavaScript file is saved.

faqs.question: Is Live Server free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Live Server is completely free and open source under the MIT license.

faqs.question: Who created Live Server?
faqs.answer: It was created by Indian developer Ritwick Dey and first published to the VS Code Marketplace in June 2017.

faqs.question: How do you start Live Server?
faqs.answer: Click Go Live in the VS Code status bar, right-click an HTML file and choose Open with Live Server, or use the command palette or keyboard shortcuts.

faqs.question: Does Live Server work with dynamic pages like PHP or Node.js?
faqs.answer: The core extension serves static files; dynamic backend live reload requires the companion Live Server Web Extension browser extension.

faqs.question: What are alternatives to Live Server?
faqs.answer: Alternatives include Microsoft's built-in Live Preview extension, the community fork Five Server, and framework-native dev servers like Vite or webpack-dev-server.

use_cases.title: Learning HTML, CSS and JavaScript
use_cases.description: Students and coding bootcamp learners use Live Server to see instant visual feedback while working through web development tutorials.

use_cases.title: Static site prototyping
use_cases.description: Front-end developers use Live Server to quickly prototype static HTML/CSS layouts without setting up a full build pipeline.

use_cases.title: Quick local testing across devices
use_cases.description: Developers use Live Server's remote LAN access to preview a page on a phone or tablet during responsive design testing.

features: Local dev server with live reload

enrichment.short_description: Multi-channel help desk and live chat software from Quality Unit combining ticketing, chat, call center and social support in one dashboard.
enrichment.pricing_model: Subscription (30-day free trial, no permanent free tier)
enrichment.seo_title: LiveAgent Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: LiveAgent review covering help desk and live chat features, pricing plans, pros and cons, and alternatives like Zendesk, Freshdesk and LiveChat.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: LiveAgent is a multi-channel help desk and live chat platform developed by Quality Unit, a software company founded in 2004 by Andrej Harsani and Viktor Zeman in Bratislava, Slovakia. The founders originally built the live chat functionality to solve their own support problem, needing a way to help customers of their affiliate-marketing product Post Affiliate Pro in real time, and that internal tool was later spun out and expanded. In 2011, LiveAgent underwent a major overhaul that transformed it from a standalone live chat widget into a full multi-channel help desk, and the company has continued building on that foundation for over a decade, marking its 20th anniversary in 2024. Quality Unit remains headquartered in Bratislava, with additional offices in Kyiv, Ukraine and New York City, and reports more than 100 employees and 40,000-plus customers representing roughly 150 million end users worldwide. The core product unifies email, live chat, voice through call center and IVR, and social and messaging channels including Facebook, Instagram, X, Viber, Telegram and WhatsApp on higher tiers into a single shared ticketing inbox, so support agents can respond to a customer regardless of which channel they used to reach out. Beyond the omnichannel inbox, LiveAgent includes a built-in knowledge base and customer self-service portal, contact forms, automation rules, canned responses, agent performance reporting, service-level agreement enforcement, proactive chat invitations, and, on more recent plans, AI features including an AI Answer Assistant and AI chatbot. LiveAgent markets itself heavily on feature count, claiming over 175 individual help desk and live chat features, and on integration breadth, connecting to CRMs, e-commerce platforms and hundreds of third-party apps. Pricing follows a straightforward per-agent, per-month subscription model with no perpetual free plan, though every tier includes a 30-day free trial requiring no credit card. The Small Business plan starts at roughly $15 per agent per month billed annually and covers ticketing, live chat, the knowledge base, customer portal, contact forms, automation rules and the AI Answer Assistant. The Medium Business plan, around $29 per agent per month, adds the call center and IVR module, time-based automation rules, reporting, proactive chat and SLAs. The Large Business plan, around $49 per agent per month, adds single sign-on, custom agent roles, time tracking, audit logs and the full set of social and messaging channel integrations. An Enterprise plan, priced around $69 per agent per month, layers on a dedicated account manager, priority support, assisted onboarding for channel integrations and custom branding. All plans and API access are included without extra add-on fees, which LiveAgent positions as a differentiator against competitors that charge separately for API access or advanced automation. LiveAgent's target customers are small and mid-size businesses and support teams that want an all-in-one help desk without the enterprise pricing of Zendesk or Freshdesk, but that need more depth than a pure live-chat widget like Tawk.to or Crisp can offer. It is frequently positioned in third-party reviews as a lower-cost alternative to Zendesk and Freshdesk aimed at cost-conscious SMBs and growing support teams. Its most direct competitors are Zendesk, Freshdesk, Help Scout, Zoho Desk and, notably, LiveChat from the Polish company Text S.A., formerly LiveChat Software, a separate and unaffiliated company with a similarly named but distinct product that focuses more narrowly on live chat and AI customer-service tooling rather than a full multi-channel ticketing suite. LiveAgent differentiates itself primarily on breadth, bundling chat, ticketing, call center and social channels together at a relatively low per-agent price, and on its long operating history, since Quality Unit has been building support software since 2004, longer than most SMB-focused help desk vendors, as evidence of platform maturity accumulated over two decades.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: LiveAgent is a multi-channel help desk and live chat platform built by Quality Unit, founded in 2004 in Bratislava, Slovakia by Andrej Harsani and Viktor Zeman.
It is a separate and unaffiliated company and product from LiveChat, the similarly named Polish live chat company; the two are frequently confused but have no corporate relationship.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: LiveAgent unifies email, live chat, call center/IVR and social and messaging channels into a single shared ticketing inbox with over 175 claimed features.
It includes a knowledge base, customer portal, automation rules, SLA enforcement, agent performance reporting and AI-powered answer assistance and chatbots.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: LiveAgent uses per-agent monthly subscription pricing across four tiers, from Small Business at around $15/agent/month up to Enterprise at around $69/agent/month, all billed annually with a 30-day free trial.
API access and integrations are included in every tier at no extra cost, unlike some competitors that charge separately for these.

faqs.answer: LiveAgent is a multi-channel help desk and live chat platform from Quality Unit that unifies email, chat, voice and social support into one ticketing system.

faqs.question: Is LiveAgent the same company as LiveChat?
faqs.answer: No. LiveAgent is made by Quality Unit in Bratislava, Slovakia, while LiveChat is made by Text S.A. in Wrocław, Poland; they are separate, unrelated companies.

faqs.question: How much does LiveAgent cost?
faqs.answer: Plans start around $15 per agent per month billed annually for Small Business, rising to around $69 per agent per month for Enterprise.

faqs.question: Does LiveAgent offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, LiveAgent offers a 30-day free trial on all plans with no credit card required.

faqs.question: When was LiveAgent founded?
faqs.answer: Quality Unit, the company behind LiveAgent, was founded in 2004 in Bratislava, Slovakia; the help desk product was substantially overhauled in 2011.

faqs.question: What are LiveAgent's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Main competitors include Zendesk, Freshdesk, Help Scout, Zoho Desk and LiveChat.

enrichment.short_description: Live chat and AI customer service platform from Text S.A. (formerly LiveChat Software), publicly traded on the Warsaw Stock Exchange. Separate company from LiveAgent.
enrichment.seo_title: LiveChat Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: LiveChat review covering features, pricing plans, company history as a public company, and how it differs from the unrelated LiveAgent platform.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: LiveChat is a live chat and customer service platform developed and sold by Text S.A., a publicly traded Polish company previously known as LiveChat Software S.A. It should not be confused with LiveAgent, the Slovakia-based help desk company built by Quality Unit; the two are separate, unrelated businesses that happen to have similar product names in the same market category. LiveChat Software was founded in 2002 in Wrocław, Poland by Mariusz Ciepły, Maciej Jarzębowski and Jakub Sitarz, but the company did not begin selling its product as a SaaS offering on the global market until 2010. Its ownership passed through several hands over the following decade: Capital Partners S.A. acquired a stake in 2006, that stake was later sold to Gadu-Gadu S.A., and the company was owned by GG Network, part of South Africa's Naspers Group, between 2008 and 2011, before the founders and management executed a buyout in 2011 to regain control. LiveChat Software went public on the Warsaw Stock Exchange in April 2014 under the ticker LVC, and in 2023 the parent company rebranded itself from LiveChat Software to Text to reflect a broader push into AI-driven customer service and e-commerce products, though the LiveChat product itself kept its name. Text is headquartered in Wrocław, Poland, employs roughly 280 people, and reported trailing-twelve-month revenue of roughly 90 million dollars and annual recurring revenue approaching 90 million dollars as of mid-2026, making it one of the larger and more financially transparent companies in this category thanks to its public listing. The LiveChat product itself is a customer-facing chat widget paired with an agent-side inbox, and the company has increasingly layered AI into that inbox; its LiveChat 2.0 positioning highlights AI agents working alongside human agents inside a unified inbox, along with a built-in revenue-attribution counter that ties chat conversations to sales outcomes. Core capabilities include Text Intelligence analytics, visitor tracking, customizable chat widgets, canned responses, staffing and workload prediction tools, SMS and Apple Messages for Business channels on higher tiers, and reporting ranging from basic dashboards to advanced agent-performance analytics. LiveChat is also part of a broader product family from the same parent company, which separately sells ChatBot, a chatbot builder, and HelpDesk, a ticketing tool, giving Text a multi-product suite that customers can combine. Pricing is subscription-based with a 14-day free trial and no credit card required upfront. The Starter plan is priced at $19 per month billed annually for a single user and includes Text Intelligence, tracking of up to 100 visitors, one recurring campaign, 60 days of chat history and basic widget customization. The Team plan, at $49 per person per month annually, is aimed at full-time support teams and adds tracking of up to 400 visitors, unlimited campaigns, unlimited chat history, full widget customization and basic reporting. The Business plan, at $79 per person per month annually, targets dedicated customer service departments and adds tracking of up to 1,000 visitors, a work scheduler, staffing prediction, on-demand and advanced reporting, and SMS and Apple Messages for Business channels. An Enterprise plan offers custom pricing with white-label widgets, HIPAA compliance, SSO, dedicated onboarding and engineering support, and negotiable payment terms. LiveChat's customer base spans more than 35,000 companies globally, skewing toward e-commerce and mid-market customer service teams that want a polished, well-supported chat widget backed by a financially stable, publicly audited vendor. Its principal competitors include Intercom, Zendesk Chat, Tidio, Crisp and, in the help-desk-adjacent category, LiveAgent and Freshchat. LiveChat differentiates itself through its public-company transparency and stability, its integrated AI-plus-human inbox model, and its multi-product ecosystem of LiveChat, ChatBot and HelpDesk sold by the same parent, letting customers consolidate chat, automation and ticketing under one vendor relationship.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: LiveChat is a live chat and AI customer service platform made by Text S.A. (formerly LiveChat Software S.A.), a Polish company founded in 2002 and publicly traded on the Warsaw Stock Exchange since 2014.
It is a separate, unrelated company from LiveAgent, the Slovakia-based help desk vendor; despite similar names the two have no corporate connection.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: LiveChat combines a customer-facing chat widget with an AI-plus-human agent inbox, Text Intelligence analytics, visitor tracking, staffing prediction and a revenue-attribution counter.
It is part of a broader product suite from the same parent company that also includes ChatBot and HelpDesk, letting customers consolidate chat, automation and ticketing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: LiveChat is priced per person per month with a 14-day free trial, starting at $19/month for Starter and rising to $79/person/month for Business, with custom Enterprise pricing above that.
Higher tiers add visitor tracking limits, staffing prediction, advanced reporting, SMS/Apple Messages for Business, white labeling and HIPAA compliance.

enrichment.short_description: Browser-based webinar and video engagement platform for hosting live, on-demand and recorded events without downloads or plugins.
enrichment.seo_title: Livestorm Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Livestorm review covering webinar features, attendee-credit pricing, free plan limits, and alternatives like Zoom Webinars and Demio.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Livestorm is a browser-based webinar and video engagement platform founded in 2016 by Gilles Bertaux, who set out to build a video events product that removed the friction of plugins, downloads and unreliable connections that plagued earlier webinar software. The company is headquartered in Paris, France, and has grown to roughly 100 to 120 employees. By 2020 Livestorm had reported approximately 11.5 million dollars in annual revenue and roughly 3,500 customers, figures that have grown substantially since. The defining architectural choice behind Livestorm is that it runs entirely inside the browser, for both hosts and attendees, with no downloads or plugins required, a deliberate contrast to legacy webinar tools that historically required desktop client installs. On top of that browser-native video infrastructure, Livestorm bundles the full workflow a marketing or events team needs to run a webinar: registration page and landing page builders, automated email reminder and follow-up sequences, live and on-demand session hosting, live captioning and automatic transcription, in-session engagement tools such as chat, polls, Q&A, emoji reactions and stage invitations to bring attendees on screen, post-event analytics and reporting, and more recently AI content repurposing that extracts material from a webinar transcript and reformats it into other content types for reuse across marketing channels. It integrates with CRM and marketing automation tools including HubSpot and Zapier at lower tiers and Salesforce, Marketo, Pardot and Microsoft Dynamics at the Enterprise tier, and it holds GDPR compliance and ISO 27001 certification, which matters to the B2B marketing and sales teams that make up much of its customer base. Livestorm's pricing shifted in late 2021 from fixed monthly subscription tiers to a usage-based, attendee-credit model, which remains in place today. There is a permanent Free plan intended for evaluation rather than production use: it supports unlimited events and unlimited team moderators but caps each live session at 20 minutes and 30 attendees. Paid usage begins with the Pro plan, billed through annually purchased attendee credits priced at roughly 2.50 to 3.00 dollars per credit, where one credit is consumed per unique participant per session within a 12-month period, whether they attend live, join late or watch a recording, and team members themselves do not consume credits. Pro supports up to 3,000 live attendees per event, four-hour session length, unlimited events and recordings, unlimited team members, and the standard integration and multilingual feature set; in practice this tier is commonly marketed starting around 99 to 110 dollars per month depending on credit volume purchased. An Enterprise plan, priced individually through a custom contract, raises the session length cap to 12 hours, unlocks the enterprise CRM integrations, adds custom analytics and reporting, a dedicated Customer Success Manager, live event shadowing support, and VIP multilingual support with a service-level agreement. Livestorm's target audience is primarily B2B marketing, sales enablement, HR and learning and development, and customer education teams that need to run recurring webinars, product demos, onboarding sessions or virtual summits without dedicating engineering resources to video infrastructure. Its core competitors include Zoom Webinars, GoTo Webinar, Demio, WebinarJam, ON24 and Webex Events, alongside newer AI-driven entrants like WebinarKit. Livestorm differentiates itself through its no-download, fully browser-based architecture, its focus on the full marketing funnel around a webinar with landing pages, email sequences and post-event analytics bundled in rather than sold as separate tools, and its usage-based attendee-credit pricing, which can be more cost-efficient than flat per-host-seat pricing for organizations running occasional large events rather than constant smaller ones.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Livestorm is a browser-based webinar and video engagement platform founded in 2016 by Gilles Bertaux and headquartered in Paris, France, with roughly 100 to 120 employees.
It runs entirely in the browser for both hosts and attendees with no downloads or plugins, distinguishing it from legacy desktop-client webinar tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Livestorm bundles registration pages, automated email sequences, live captioning and transcription, in-session engagement tools like polls and Q&A, and AI content repurposing from webinar transcripts.
It integrates with HubSpot and Zapier broadly, and with Salesforce, Marketo, Pardot and Microsoft Dynamics on the Enterprise tier, and holds GDPR and ISO 27001 compliance.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Livestorm offers a permanent Free plan capped at 20-minute sessions and 30 attendees, while paid tiers use an annually purchased attendee-credit model priced at roughly $2.50-3.00 per credit.
The Pro plan supports up to 3,000 live attendees and 4-hour sessions, while Enterprise adds 12-hour sessions, enterprise CRM integrations and a dedicated Customer Success Manager at custom pricing.

enrichment.short_description: Lightweight, self-hosted, privacy-friendly web analytics tool written in Rust that runs as a single binary with no database required.
enrichment.seo_title: Liwan Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Liwan review of this open-source, self-hosted, single-binary web analytics tool built in Rust, plus alternatives like Plausible and Litlyx.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Liwan is a lightweight, self-hosted, privacy-first web analytics tool created by German developer Henry Gressmann, known in open-source circles by the handle explodingcamera. The project was introduced publicly in 2024, documented in Gressmann's own blog post announcing it as a fast, simple alternative to heavier self-hosted analytics platforms. Unlike most of the other tools in this comparison set, Liwan is not a venture-backed company with a formal headquarters or a large team; it is an independently maintained open-source project distributed on GitHub under the explodingcamera/liwan repository, with its creator based in Germany. It should also be distinguished from Litlyx, another privacy-focused analytics tool in the same broad category but built by a different team as a commercial, AI-augmented product; Liwan is deliberately minimal and infrastructure-focused rather than feature-rich or AI-driven. Liwan's defining technical characteristic is its architecture: it is written in Rust and built on the tokio asynchronous runtime, and it compiles down to a single self-contained binary with no external database dependency. This means deployment consists of downloading and running one executable, or running the equivalent Docker image, rather than provisioning a database server, a cache layer and an application server as many analytics platforms require. The project explicitly markets this efficiency by noting it can run comfortably on a low-cost VPS, an old Mac Mini repurposed as a home server, or even a Raspberry Pi, positioning Liwan at the extreme lightweight end of the self-hosted analytics spectrum. On privacy, Liwan follows the same no-cookie, no-cross-site-tracking, no-persistent-identifier model used by other privacy-first analytics tools such as Plausible, Fathom and Umami: it does not set tracking cookies, does not build cross-site visitor profiles, and keeps all collected data on infrastructure the operator controls rather than sending it to a third-party vendor. The dashboard covers the standard analytics surface, including page views, visitor counts grouped into visitor groups, and referrer sources, and the tracking pipeline automatically filters out obvious bots and crawlers to keep reported numbers closer to real human traffic. Because the project emphasizes simplicity over feature breadth, it does not attempt to replicate more advanced product-analytics capabilities like funnels, session replay or AI-generated reporting that some commercial competitors, including Litlyx, offer. There is no commercial pricing model: Liwan is free and open source to self-host, and the project's own hosted demo, available at demo.liwan.dev, exists to let prospective users try the dashboard before deploying their own instance rather than as a paid managed offering. This makes Liwan effectively free for anyone willing to run and maintain their own server, with the tradeoff, common to all self-hosted software, that the operator is responsible for installation, updates, backups and uptime, since there is no vendor-run cloud SaaS tier and no commercial support contract available. Liwan's target audience is developers, indie hackers and privacy-conscious organizations that already self-host other infrastructure and want an analytics tool that is trivially easy to deploy alongside it, without the operational overhead of running a database-backed analytics stack like Matomo or PostHog. It particularly appeals to technically sophisticated users comfortable working directly with a GitHub project and its documentation rather than a polished commercial onboarding flow. Its closest competitors are other self-hosted, privacy-first analytics tools: Plausible Analytics and Umami, both of which require a database unlike Liwan, Fathom Analytics and Simple Analytics, both hosted-only commercial products, and Litlyx, a commercial AI-augmented alternative that also offers self-hosting but layers on a much larger feature set and a paid cloud tier. Liwan differentiates itself primarily through radical operational simplicity, a single binary with no database and a minimal resource footprint, at the cost of the richer feature sets, AI capabilities and managed hosting options that larger, better-funded competitors provide.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Liwan is a lightweight, self-hosted, privacy-first web analytics tool created by German developer Henry Gressmann and introduced publicly in 2024 as an independent open-source project.
It is written in Rust, compiles to a single self-contained binary with no database dependency, and can run on infrastructure as small as a Raspberry Pi.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Liwan tracks page views, visitor groups and referrers with automatic bot and crawler filtering, while avoiding cookies, cross-site tracking and persistent identifiers.
It deliberately skips advanced product-analytics features like funnels or AI reporting to stay minimal and easy to operate, distinguishing it from feature-rich competitors like Litlyx.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Liwan is entirely free and open source; there is no paid managed hosting tier, and the hosted demo exists only to let users try the dashboard before self-deploying.
Operators are responsible for installing, updating and maintaining their own instance, typical of self-hosted open-source software.

features.title: Single-Binary Deployment
features.description: Compiles to one self-contained executable with no external database required.

features.title: Rust and Tokio Architecture
features.description: Built in Rust with the tokio async runtime for speed and low resource usage.

features.title: Cookieless Privacy Model
features.description: No cookies, no cross-site tracking and no persistent visitor identifiers.

features.title: Bot and Crawler Filtering
features.description: Automatically filters obvious bots and crawlers from reported traffic.

features.title: Page Views and Referrer Tracking
features.description: Core analytics dashboard covering page views, visitor groups and referrer sources.

features.title: Minimal Infrastructure Footprint
features.description: Runs on a cheap VPS, an old Mac Mini or a Raspberry Pi with minimal setup.

features.title: Open Source on GitHub
features.description: Fully open-source codebase available at explodingcamera/liwan for self-hosting and contribution.

features.title: Hosted Demo
features.description: A public hosted demo at demo.liwan.dev lets users try the dashboard before self-deploying.

pros: Completely free and open source with no paid tier required
Extremely lightweight, single-binary deployment with no database
Strong privacy model with no cookies or persistent identifiers
Can run on very low-cost hardware including a Raspberry Pi
Minimal, focused feature set is easy to understand and operate
cons: No managed hosted SaaS option, so operators must self-host and maintain it themselves
Small, solo-maintained project with no dedicated support team
Lacks advanced features like funnels, session replay or AI reporting
Smaller community and ecosystem than more established tools like Plausible
Documentation and polish are more limited than commercially backed competitors

faqs.question: What is Liwan?
faqs.answer: Liwan is a lightweight, self-hosted, privacy-first web analytics tool written in Rust that runs as a single binary with no database required.

faqs.question: Is Liwan free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Liwan is entirely free and open source; there is no paid managed hosting tier.

faqs.question: Who created Liwan?
faqs.answer: Liwan was created by German developer Henry Gressmann, known as explodingcamera, and introduced publicly in 2024.

faqs.question: Does Liwan require a database?
faqs.answer: No, Liwan compiles to a single self-contained binary with no external database dependency.

faqs.question: Is Liwan the same as Litlyx?
faqs.answer: No, Liwan and Litlyx are separate, unrelated privacy-analytics projects; Liwan is a minimal open-source self-hosted tool while Litlyx is a commercial, AI-augmented product.

faqs.question: What hardware can run Liwan?
faqs.answer: Liwan is lightweight enough to run on a cheap VPS, an old Mac Mini or even a Raspberry Pi.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted infrastructure analytics
use_cases.description: Developers who already self-host their own applications add Liwan alongside them to track site traffic without adopting a new database-backed stack.

use_cases.title: Low-resource deployments
use_cases.description: Teams running services on minimal hardware like a Raspberry Pi or small VPS use Liwan because it needs no database and has a tiny resource footprint.
use_cases.audience: Homelab operators and small teams with limited infrastructure

use_cases.title: Privacy-first minimal tracking
use_cases.description: Privacy-conscious site owners use Liwan to get basic page view and referrer analytics without cookies or persistent visitor identifiers.

features: Page views, visitor groups and referrer tracking
Full data ownership on your own infrastructure
